summary: France's second-largest AOC wine region, split into a steep, granite-soiled North making single-variety Syrah reds and Viognier/Marsanne/Roussanne whites, and a broad, warm South built on Grenache-led GSM blends grown on galets roulés (rolled pebbles). Styles range from age-worthy, peppery Northern Syrah and rich Southern reds to dry rosés and rare sweet Muscats.
classification: Three-tier hierarchy: regional Côtes du Rhône AOC at the base, then Côtes du Rhône Villages (with named villages), then the named Crus at the top. Northern Crus: Côte-Rôtie, Condrieu, Château-Grillet, Hermitage, Crozes-Hermitage, Saint-Joseph, Cornas, Saint-Péray. Southern Crus: Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Gigondas, Vacqueyras, Cairanne, Rasteau, Beaumes-de-Venise, Vinsobres, Lirac and the rosé-only Tavel.

summary: Mediterranean region world-famous for pale, dry, herb-scented rosé built on Grenache, Cinsault, Syrah and Mourvèdre, accounting for the bulk of production. Beyond rosé it makes serious Mourvèdre-driven reds (above all in Bandol) and distinctive whites from Clairette, Rolle (Vermentino) and Ugni Blanc.
classification: Headed by the large Côtes de Provence AOC (with named sub-zones such as Sainte-Victoire, La Londe, Fréjus), plus Coteaux d'Aix-en-Provence and Coteaux Varois en Provence. The prestige reds come from small coastal crus: Bandol (Mourvèdre), Cassis (white-focused), Bellet (above Nice) and tiny Palette near Aix.

summary: France's largest vineyard expanse, sweeping the Mediterranean arc from the Rhône delta to the Spanish border, producing predominantly Grenache-, Syrah-, Mourvèdre- and Carignan-based reds and rosés plus aromatic whites. Roussillon, Catalan in culture, is the heartland of vins doux naturels (fortified sweet wines) such as Banyuls and Maury alongside concentrated dry reds.
classification: A patchwork of AOCs under the umbrella Languedoc AOC, with rising terroir crus and named sub-zones: Pic Saint-Loup, Terrasses du Larzac, La Clape, Faugères, Saint-Chinian, Minervois (and cru Minervois-La Livinière), Corbières (and Corbières-Boutenac), Fitou, plus Limoux for sparkling. In Roussillon: Côtes du Roussillon (Villages), the dry red cru Collioure, and fortified VDN appellations Banyuls, Maury and Rivesaltes. Much top-quality wine is sold as IGP Pays d'Oc.

summary: A mountainous Mediterranean island whose wines are defined by indigenous grapes: the Sangiovese-related Nielluccio and the peppery, aromatic Sciaccarello for reds and rosés, and Vermentino (Vermentinu) for crisp, saline whites. Styles span structured age-worthy reds, fresh rosés and the rare sweet Muscat du Cap Corse.
classification: Nine AOCs: Patrimonio (the island's first, 1968) and Ajaccio as the two flagship standalone crus, the sweet Muscat du Cap Corse, plus the regional Vin de Corse AOC with five geographic village denominations: Calvi, Figari, Sartène, Porto-Vecchio and Coteaux du Cap Corse. Much expressive wine is bottled as IGP Île de Beauté.
